data.title: MLPI Vs. AMLP: Why NEOS Is The New Leader Among Midstream ETFs
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The NEOS MLP & Energy Infrastructure High Income ETF (MLPI) caught my attention immediately when the new product from the NEOS team was launched. Also, after looking at and comparing similar funds investing in midstream assets, which readers often recommended I pay special attention to, MLPI stood out. The lack of a track record, though, kept me from taking a practical interest in this asset. Since we now have the first results after three months, which allow us to assess MLPI’s performance, here’s my attempt to compare it with the sector’s benchmark—the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP).

Midstream assets have proven to be more volatile over this short period, which is primarily due to rising energy prices. Since we are currently in a bull market, we can immediately find an answer to the question regarding whether NEOS funds are subject to a "growth ceiling." Comparing the total returns of the MLPI and the AMLP over a 3-month period shows that the former outperformed the latter by 4.36%. Therefore, in practice, these theoretical disadvantages are offset by the effective actions of NEOS portfolio managers. The comparison of MLPI and AMLP in this article confirms that the former fund deserves the status of "top performer" among MLP and Midstream assets.

Fundamental Similarities Between MLPI and AMLP

Every investor should understand the actual structure of these investment vehicles, as although MLPI and AMLP are two popular ETFs that invest in MLP assets, their internal workings differ significantly. Nevertheless, it is worth noting their common "genetic code" first.

  • Firstly, the two funds are pure-play MLP holdings in the midstream sector. Because of this, the structure is classified as a C-Corporation.
  • Secondly, the funds' assets do not include any companies in the E&P sector. Therefore, there is 67% overlap in their holdings. As a result, the price movements of their shares are highly correlated.
  • Thirdly, investors who hold shares in these funds receive Form 1099-DIV instead of the complex Form K-1. MLPI and AMLP can therefore be held in IRA/401k retirement accounts without UBTI issues.
  • Finally, these funds are primarily suited for investors focused on high dividend income who want to invest in assets with relatively low correlation to the S&P 500.

Fundamental Differences Between MLPI and AMLP

An in-depth analysis of these ETFs reveals the criteria by which they should be distinguished. Firstly, their asset ownership structure differs: the NEOS managers employ an active management strategy that incorporates a covered call strategy. A distinctive feature of this strategy is the sale of out-of-the-money options on selected MLPs, generating additional returns from the option premium. In this regard, the total return on MLPI is higher than that of AMLP. Although when we talk about MLPI, what we mean first and foremost is investing in MLPs, it actually includes a mixed portfolio where the share of partnerships is no more than 25%, and the remaining 75% consists of shares in energy corporations, among which are Canadian issuers.

By the way, it makes MLPI similar to another fund, the Global X MLP & Energy Infrastructure ETF (MLPX), the one I compared to AMLP earlier. It was because of the diversified portfolio structure that I chose MLPX, thinking it would have a higher total return by 2026. From the time of publication on January 5, 2026, the total return for MLPX was 24.94%, whereas AMLP’s was only 15.41%. I think this 9.53% advantage is due precisely to portfolio diversification, a feature that is also typical of MLPI. Because NEOS has a wider selection of managers, it can significantly diversify its portfolio across assets; it has 27 holdings in its portfolio, while the top 10 assets account for 63.34% of the portfolio.

Portfolio managers at AMLP, in contrast, favor passive portfolio management, which tracks an index comprising the largest MLPs. I think this management approach is less effective since it doesn't leave room for maneuver or flexibility when rebalancing the portfolio during the market's most volatile periods, like February-March 2026. As the choice is limited to midstream companies in this sector, this portfolio is less diversified (only 13 holdings) and significantly more concentrated (the top 10 assets account for 93.67%).

There is a more significant difference between the funds in their dividend policies, which favor MLPI. For one thing, NEOS pays monthly dividends, as opposed to quarterly dividends like AMLP. Furthermore, based on information from the MLPI website, dividend yield for the year is 14.92%, twice that of AMLP. Information provided on the Seeking Alpha website includes the yield based on 3 monthly payments.

Forming the dividend policy of the funds, on the other hand, impacts tax efficiency, with MLPI having a clear advantage over AMLP. Since the latter is a C-Corp, corporate tax (21%) is payable. In a rising market, additional tax burdens arise, and the managers of MLPI, for example, reduce these through structuring as a RIC. To maintain this tax status, a fund like NEOS must not exceed a 25% allocation to MLPs in its portfolio. Overall, based on Form 8937 for 2025, both funds have a share of ROC in their distributions; but while AMLP's share is minimal (6.68%), that of MLPI is much higher (77.09%). Because of this, MLPI shareholders can defer their tax liabilities until they sell their shares.

This results in a difference in total returns between the funds, where MLPI has the advantage. Despite the fact that data is only available starting December 17, 2025 (start of MLPI operations), it is already possible to identify a 4.36% advantage for the NEOS fund (20.98% vs. 16.62%).

The table below provides a more detailed comparison of MLPI and AMLP.

Risks of the Investment Thesis

There are risks associated with investing in MLPI and AMLP due to trends in the Midstream sector. We should keep in mind the potential risks arising from the rising yield on 10-year U.S. Treasury bonds. Such a trend could trigger capital outflows from assets like MLPI and AMLP. As of now, yield has increased to 4.456%. Should this growth trend continue and yield rise to 5%, investments in Midstream companies would become unprofitable. Among the factors driving bond yield growth are inflation risks, who are still a thing since oil and natural gas prices are crazy high. According to CME data, there's a chance the Fed might take a tougher stance when reviewing monetary policy and the key rate, something the market is already pricing in.

An additional risk stems from the volume of energy resources transported by Midstream companies, whose revenues are derived up to 90% from capacity booking. Even though oil and natural gas prices do not directly impact these companies’ financial results, an indirect effect exists due to the increased threat of stagflation and economic recession. This would lead to a collapse in demand amid persistently high inflation, further reducing capacity commitments under new contracts. Ultimately, energy transport volumes would decline, resulting in lower revenue for Midstream companies.

Conclusion

I compared the key criteria of MLPI and AMLP in this article, and concluded that although the NEOS fund is a newcomer to this market, they're the best choice among midstream ETFs. The advantages of MLPI include a structured approach to portfolio composition, higher payouts, and a lower tax burden. In addition, its portfolio is better suited for a bull market, allowing it to gain a 4.36% outperformance over AMLP in just 3 months. The NEOS management team has thus managed to overcome the "growth ceiling" barrier, allowing it to generate double-digit dividend yields without sacrificing market growth potential. Thus, my preference is for MLPI, which, regardless of the current macroeconomic risks, enjoys a number of advantages that position it as the new "gold standard" among midstream ETFs.

data.title: LENZ Therapeutics: Q4 Results Highlight A Worrying Lack Of Market Demand (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Lenz Therapeutics (LENZ) is an early-stage biotechnology company based in Solana Beach, CA. Its main product is VIZZ (aceclidine ophthalmic solution) 1.44%, marketed as ‘the first and only aceclidine-based eye drop for the treatment of presbyopia’.

The word presbyopia has ancient Greek roots, literally meaning “old man’s eye”. It is a condition where, as we age, our near-sighted vision worsens. While not everyone is diagnosed with the condition, it is considered to affect almost everyone as they age, with most people developing symptoms in their 40s or 50s. It is not a disease, but instead a natural, progressive age-related process whereby the eye loses its ability to focus on nearby objects due to a loss of flexibility and elasticity in the crystalline lens. It is why reading becomes more challenging as we age, particularly in low light.

The standard treatments for presbyopia are glasses, bifocals, or contact lenses. Collectively, this is an enormous global market; Fortune Business Insights estimates it as more than $180 billion. It is also worth noting that traditional LASIK surgery done on both eyes is not intended to fix presbyopia, as the technique is for fixing far-sighted vision. However, options like monovision/multifocal ablation which use lasers to set one eye for near vision and the other for far vision can fix the symptoms temporarily. I say temporarily because, again, presbyopia is age-related and progressive so, as years pass post-surgery, patients will likely require reading glasses anyway.

Estimates suggest 1.8 billion people globally have presbyopia – or 128 million Americans – but almost everyone beyond a certain age develops the condition even if they never realize it or get an official diagnosis. In any event, what is certain is that the condition is extremely common among adults in their middle/late stage of life.

Vizz received FDA approval in July 2025 as an eye-drop formula that can be used once daily to provide temporarily relief from presbyopia.

The mechanism is quite simple. The aceclidine formula gently squeezes the iris muscle to constrict the pupil and create a pin-hole effect. What this means is that there is less stray light entering the eye which creates a larger depth of focus. While the drops do not improve the flexibility of the lens – the cause of presbyopia – they nevertheless have the effect of improving the clarity of near objects by making your pupils smaller (the opposite of dilation) to about 2mm in size. As shown in the graphic below, the drops react within a few minutes with benefits lasting 8-10 hours for most people.

The market for treatment of presbyopia is estimated at $11 billion in 2025 and expected to reach $18 billion by 2036. Lenz’s product offers the potential to top into this market. In their latest earnings call, management noted they were ‘activating targeted consumer campaigns to establish VIZZ as a compelling alternative to reading glasses’, which is an ambitious objective. Their pricing model offers the potential for a big payday should the market demand materialize as the company expects. Thinking of the US pool of presbyopia sufferers (128 million), if even 1% of the market adopt the product, this would generate $1.024 billion per year using the ~$800 annual payment figure suggested by the company below. A larger (10%) penetration would yield something like $10.24 billion annually.

Management’s own estimates are for a total addressable market of around $3 billion per year, which – using these price points – suggests they can capture around 3% of the total US presbyopia sufferers. Interestingly, in their latest investor presentation, they propose a market penetration rate of 6%, which suggests they are anticipating lower ASPs going forward.

In terms of their addressable market and potential annual revenue figure, given the company’s current market cap of around $285 million, this would suggest the company is thoroughly undervalued at present. In this article, I’d like to assess their market demand in more detail and provide an update on my previous article – my first coverage of Lenz – back in December 2025.

Updating my Coverage

In my initial coverage of Lenz, I issued a buy rating based on the fairly straightforward idea that the market was underestimating the product’s potential market adoption and that the recent share-price performance – shares had fallen nearly 70% in just two months – was an overreaction to some negative news the company experienced just prior to its publication. While I was somewhat skeptical of VIZZ customer demand, I nevertheless believed that the market had sufficiently priced this risk into its shares and that VIZZ could not only steal market share from its competition – mainly Vuity from Abbvie (ABBV) (FDA approved in 2021) and Qlosi from Orasis Pharmaceuticals (approved in 2023; launched in 2025) – but also from those who use the standard treatment options of eye glasses and, especially, once-daily contact lenses. Indeed, the data suggested that VIZZ was a superior product to its competition (esp. Vuity and Qlosi) in terms of efficacy, as shown below. Also included by comparison was Yuvezzi, which was recently approved by the FDA by Tenpoint Therapeutics, and is due to launch very soon.

Vuity also had a less than spectacular take-up in its first full year of availability (2022); they filled just 190,000 prescriptions among 120,000 patients, possibly indicating that only 70,000 (56%) took it again after their first trial. Perhaps rather too optimistically, given its stronger reported efficacy and safety/tolerability, I suspected that Vuity’s inferiority was the reason for its slow take-up—a problem that VIZZ could solve. However, in doing so, I may have overestimated the product demand by believing in the company’s own market research too much, while underestimating potential customer rejections of the eye drops due to cost, safety, comfort, or other concerns.

I admit this buy recommendation was poor, with shares now trading more than 45% lower after its publication, and a full 90% below the market high in October 2025. With shares now below $9, shareholder value has been obliterated in the space of less than 6 months.

Let’s examine their most recent earnings to assess product uptake, before offering a reassessment of the market demand.

Most Recent Earnings

Lenz reported Q4 and full year 2025 earnings on March 24th. The company announced product revenue of $1.6 million through 20,000 prescriptions in Q4 alone, which was the first full quarter of sales after launching in October 2025. Lenz management expect 45,000 prescriptions in the first 6 months, which could translate to 90-100,000 prescriptions in the first twelve months if they keep up this pace. That would generate $8 million or so for the year, but this could be a big “if”. As a reminder, Abbvie’s product Vuity filled 190,000 prescriptions in the first year, so Vizz has some serious catching up to do to reach this figure.

By comparison, therefore, the figure of 20,000 was disappointing despite what management said in the earnings call about its outperformance compared to Vuity. “The market” seemed to agree with me here, with shares falling 18% the following day. The low revenue figure of $1.6 million was compounded by $39.6 million in S,G&A expenses in the quarter. According to management, 80% of this was for sales and marketing, comprised mainly of increases in headcount, primarily an 88-territory sales force, alongside several advertising campaigns. This included a direct-to-consumer (DTC) campaign involving celebrity actress Sarah Jessica Parker for this ad among several TV appearances.

These higher costs led to a Q4 net loss of $35.9 million for the quarter, or $1.16 per share, and a quarterly cash burn of $33 million. This brought the full year 2025 total net loss to $70 million.

The company paid its management and staff $3.68 million in stock-based compensation. When SBC is more than double total product sales, that raises a red flag. It is certainly something to watch for going forward. They also issued $80 million in common stock in the 4th quarter alone (though Seeking Alpha shows $149 million issued in the TTM period).

This has added meaningfully to the significant shareholder dilution already experienced to date.

The company has already burned through $354 million over the course of its history – as far as I can calculate – so they have a long way to go before getting back above water, and until they can meaningfully bring in $100 million or so in a quarter to offset their higher S,G&A expenses, SBC (which continues to rise), and secondary share offerings that dilute shareholders, I see further punishment ahead for shareholders. And let’s be frank—they are a long way from that revenue figure.

In the earnings report, management also noted it was continuing to expand its sales force from 88 to 117 territories, after having already secured ‘broad uptake by eye care professionals (“ECPs”), with over 6,500 unique prescribers in Q4 2025, on pace to grow to over 10,000 through Q1 2026’. They also noted that ‘over 55% of prescribing ECPs have written multiple times’, which is in line with what Abbvie saw in its first year, as noted above. Again, I did not think this result was particularly impressive, though it is important to give this company time to demonstrate the market demand throughout its first full year. Only then can we know what kind of customer retention rate we might see going forward. If it stays around 55%, that might suggest significantly weaker demand than expected. It means, in effect, that only half of those who try the eye drops continue to use them beyond the first initial prescription.

In this regard, let me outline my own concerns as I have spent more time now investigating this market. If they are seriously considering their VIZZ product as a disruptor to reading glasses or contact lenses, it makes sense for us to compare the pros and cons here.

Costs

In terms of cost, basic non-prescription reading glasses cost $10-30 from Walmart (WMT) or Amazon (AMZN), with even the more expensive ones from places like Warby Parker (WRBY) between $100-300. These are stylish, fashionable, comfortable and driven by high technology in the lenses, offering bifocals or Progressive Addition Lenses ("PALs") that offer a gradual transition in the lens, thereby treating distance vision, near vision, and everything in between. Even for these high-end glasses, the total for the lenses and frames would be $400-600. So long as the person wearing them looks after them, they can last several years with the same lenses, which places them as a much more cost-effective option than daily eye drops that carry recurring monthly or quarterly charges.

Of course, there are those who can’t or don’t want to wear glasses for a number of reasons including comfort or practicality. This is the small market that VIZZ is trying to tap into, but it is up against the incumbent: contact lenses. Even the high-end contact lenses with the most expensive prescriptions cost around $400-600 per year for the monthlies or up to $1000 per year for the dailies. There might be additional expenses when adding in solutions, exams and fitting, but only the solutions would be recurring. In any event, contact lenses are now in many cases covered by standard health insurance packages, and have been for years. As far as I can tell, VIZZ is not. It even highlights this on their website FAQ page (seen below). Patients wanting coverage must use their FSA or HSA plans, but these may not provide full coverage.

This means most people will have to pay either all or a portion of the ~$800 per year out-of-pocket, and I’m not sure there will be a large number of people willing to do this on a permanent basis, when there are cheaper options available. Some of course will, but we’re likely talking about a small minority of those for whom money or healthcare spending is less of a problem. CCO Shawn Olsson admitted as much in the latest earnings call when outlining their target market:

‘So we know what these patients look like, and we knew that prior to launch, right? We knew that they make over $100,000 a year. They’re mostly between 45 and 65. They’re in major city centers.’

As such, the sense that most presbyopes would be priced out is clearly anticipated, which makes much of their data on market demand – outlined below – subject to critique.

Health Risks (Serious)

I spent some time in my first write-up investigating the price drop that occurred last year as a result of news being released about an adverse event for VIZZ, whereby one patient had a full retina tear. This is a very serious problem that could potentially lead to blindness. The company acknowledged this incident as an opportunity to learn and reevaluate its prescribing methods, noting that physicians in future need to do a more comprehensive background health check to assess for high-risk patients before prescribing the medication. Nevertheless, the incident exposes the health risks that come with eye drops in general, and with those containing powerful ingredients that could cause irritation or react in some ways with a person’s physiology.

On their customer advisory information page, they offer the following warnings and precautions for VIZZ, including a retinal tear as a possibility.

According to the FDA’s Adverse Event Monitoring System database – which can be found here (search “VIZZ” by product name, then click “Listing of Cases” and “Serious”) – in total, there have been 12 reported serious adverse events among patients using the product. We can see the list below, including the full retinal tear.

Most recently, several patients have experienced vitreous detachment, which is not uncommon, often not harmful – it causes floaters in the eye or sometimes temporary flashes (photopsia) – but could lead in odd cases to a retinal tear. It is certainly serious enough to be classed, officially, as a “serious” adverse event.

These adverse events have occurred in patients prescribed the drug post-approval, and not by those who were involved in clinical trials in controlled settings. This makes the slide (below) which appears in their latest investor presentation particularly troubling to me.

While it is entirely accurate to report that ‘There were no reported retinal tears or detachments with VIZZ in clinical trials’ – because this was true – I feel it would have been more appropriate for the company to have provided more up-to-date information that does not omit facts about the serious adverse events that patients, post-approval, have experienced (including 1 retinal tear and 6 detachments). Note: the last of these serious incidents was reported on Feb 16th 2026, which may have been after the publication of their latest investor presentation. The other 5 would certainly have been known at the time though.

I was also left feeling uncomfortable with the statement ‘<4% discontinuation due to treatment-related adverse events’. Again, I’m sure this is factually correct, but given that 20% of users experienced instillation site irritation, 16% dim vision, 8% conjunctival hyperemia (specific “red eye”), 7% ocular hyperemia (general “red eye”), and 13% experienced headaches (19% of these more than "mild"), I find it very hard to believe that more than only a fraction of these people would continue using the product on a regular basis. So why the low number then?

These figures are from the trial results, and not from the general population of users post-approval. As someone who has undergone a drug trial before, I can tell you that there is a strong sense of responsibility that comes with it. Patients are perhaps more likely to tolerate any manageable health concerns as they are considered temporary; they may feel a sense of guilt if they discontinue use before the trial concludes. Among the general population, when not being trialed, however, I would suggest the discontinuation rate would be much higher. They would feel no obligation to continue using the medication if it wasn’t easily tolerated, especially given the other options seem very reasonable and even more tolerable in the case of glasses. Indeed, the 55% repeat prescription figure cited above suggests that I might be correct here. Call it a hunch. Indeed, Lenz admitted that only 75% of their 223 phase 1/2 trial participants would continue to use VIZZ after the study. But let’s remember that this was Lenz researchers collecting the data rather than someone independent.

In any case, the company did not disclose in their investor presentation that a dozen users have reported experiencing a serious adverse reaction. Again, FDA approval came in July last year, so the company had 7 months of post-approval data from thousands of users by the time they put together their slide deck.

Health Risks (Mild)

Turning to dim vision, which carried a 16% precedence rate (with 8% of these more than “mild”), this is also a concern given that night-driving could be impacted by using VIZZ. This is likely to be a more serious problem during winter months, and especially in the northern US, when days are shorter and nightfall comes earlier. If someone uses the eye drops at 9am for example, the effects could last 8-10 hours, meaning that by the time they drive home for work, it is dark out. The pinhole effect created by the eyedrops mean that less light enters the eye. While this helps the eye to focus on near objects, it can be a problem when there is less natural or ambient light around. I would suspect, based on simple logic, that anyone experiencing dim vision and difficulty with night-driving as a consequence of using VIZZ would either stop using the product or cut down its use during the winter months, which would of course hurt both sales and the product’s reputation/usefulness.

In all, it must be said that glasses bring next to no health risks such as these. Contact lenses have been known to cause eye infections, conjunctivitis (pink eye), corneal ulcers and vision loss in extreme cases, but proper care, maintenance, and hygiene relieve almost all these risks. This presents VIZZ as a higher risk solution to presbyopia.

Taking the differences in cost and safety together, I posit that it is likely to be only a very small proportion of the population that will feel VIZZ eyedrops are the best solution for them. This is my main concern for their market; simply put, it is not as large as Lenz management think it is. So, while – in the best-case scenario – they may come to corner their market, stripping away any dominance that Vuity or Qlosi has at this point, if too few people want their product due to high cost and health risks, it won’t matter if theirs is the best. As a friend of mine in the investing community reminds me: if you own the only movie theatre in town but no one wants to see a movie, your dominant position is irrelevant. Even if Lenz come to control the supply, their has to be a demand for them to profit from it.

While I was willing to give them the benefit of the doubt in my last review – they had not delivered any product sales at that point – the most recent earnings highlighted what I feared most: little market demand for their product.

Of course, this is compounded because as of 2026 Lenz is a one-trick pony. For Q4 2025, their R&D expenses fell to $0 as they have gone “all in” on their one product with nothing in the pipeline. This presents an enormous risk. Without sufficient sales of VIZZ, they have nothing to fall back on.

Management noted progress with international expansion, forming a relationship with Lotus Pharmaceutical helping to provide an 8-country license in Southeast Asia and with Lunatus to focus on the Middle East. They submitted applications to the European health authority and have plans for UK and Canada going forward. CFO Daniel Chevallard announced in the call:

‘In total, 5 ex U.S. NDA or equivalent submissions have been completed, and we anticipate over 10 to be completed by the end of this year with multiple potential approvals possible in 2027.’

This is positive news but so much of their long-term potential is riding on market demand in the US, its most important market to penetrate at this stage. On this point, I raise some concerns.

Reassessing Market Demand

According to a Lenz commissioned survey of 1,358 presbyopes, over 60% ‘would consider an eye drop solution’. From this statistic and their estimation of the potential size of the market – 128 million presbyopes in the US – Lenz management have speculated a $3 billion market opportunity and ‘blockbuster potential’ with VIZZ.

Lenz suggested the proposed 6% adoption rate was ‘conservative’, yet this represents 8 million people in the US, which is actually an enormous number of people. As investors, we should ask the blunt question: would 8 million people prefer to ditch their glasses or contact lenses, which are significantly cheaper and carry fewer health considerations, for a once-daily eye drop that provides temporary relief for an age-related, progressive condition that has already been “fixed” by other methods for decades? If so, should we short EssilorLuxottica (ESLOY) and Warby Parker shares? It would make sense to if we believed these projections, but, personally, I do not.

My confidence (of lack of) stems from a reassessment of market demand based on Vuity revenue. If a decent-sized market existed for VIZZ, we would see glimpses of this in Vuity revenues, as one of only two other eye-drop medications on the market (the other being Qlosi, launched in April 2025). In their latest earnings release, Abbvie did not mention Vuity at all in their 12-page prepared remarks and financial statements; nor was it discussed on the earnings call itself or in the Q&A session. I went back and scanned several earnings call transcripts and annual reports, and nothing about Vuity was said, going back several quarters. Then I came across an article that noted:

‘In September 2022, AbbVie decided to reduce sales and marketing activities around Vuity, contributing to a pre-tax impairment charge of $770 million in the third quarter of 2022.’

This showed up in Abbvie’s Q4 and full year 2022 earnings and was discussed in their 10-K annual report, noting:

‘In September 2022, the company made a strategic decision to reduce ongoing sales and marketing investment related to Vuity, an on-market product to treat presbyopia.’

One can only assume the product was not selling well, which then begs the following questions in relation to VIZZ:

  • Can we assume that the poor performance of Vuity was related to the product itself and not weak market demand for the product line in general?
  • How much confidence should we have that VIZZ will outperform Vuity and that the former will buck the trend in this regard, providing the market solution that Vuity was not able to?

Indeed, the differences between the two products were not vast. Certainly, Vuity was less efficacious (did not last as long; required two drops per day instead of one) and had a superior safety profile, but this does not necessarily translate to strong market demand. I keep coming back to this point because it is crucial.

Lenz noted in their latest earnings call that they anticipate their DTC campaign will take several quarters to roll out and ‘translate into prescription trends’. CEO Evert Schimmelpennink added:

‘What we are seeing today are encouraging early indicators of that process, and we would expect a more meaningful impact on script volume as we move into the second half of the year.’

The company noted that 5 months into launch, the unaided awareness of their product stands at 79%, which seems rather high to me. But the figure might be working against them, because if so many people know about the product but the prescriptions numbers are so low, then that might be the clearest indication yet that the market demand is just not there.

Investors must keep a close eye on these figures, but I have strong reservations here. I do not believe the company has a moat around its business, or any kind of competitive advantage, therefore little pricing power. Moreover, I remain unconvinced by the market size. I have no reason to believe that Lenz will get anywhere near the 6% adoption rate (8 million users), which would afford the company a revaluation and send shares upward.

Financial and Valuation Considerations

Lenz ended the quarter with $292 million in cash and equivalents and next to no debt (just $350,000), which gives them a decent runway of something like 2-3 years if the current quarterly cash burn is sustained. So, while the company has more cash than their entire market cap – cash per share of $9.40 with a current share price of $8.94 (as of March 26th closing price) – the market is clearly pricing in this weak demand already. This is a classic value trap and will continue to be viewed as such until they can mitigate the one, major concern here: weaker than expected market demand for VIZZ.

Lenz carries a PB ratio around 1 and a current PS ratio of 14.7 and an EV/sales ratio around 11. It is hardly cheap from a revenue perspective, but certainly looks cheap from a balance sheet (book value) perspective. However, this is misleading given the company will almost certainly continue to burn through cash over the next year and possibly into 2027, thereby impacting shareholder equity. In fact, the path to reaching FCF positive might take several years by which time the company may have to dilute shareholders still further with additional debt financing.

Short interest is now around 28% according to Seeking Alpha, which is very high and should raise some red flags for investors, prospective and current. Some insider buying in November last year (CFO Daniel Chevallard and director James Mccollum) helped propel the stock higher but almost immediate market selling from another insider (CCO Shawn Olsson) sends a questionable message to the investment community. Shares were trading above $20 at that point. Now they are valued at less than half that, but they have not been bought up by management.

Taking their most recent earnings results into consideration, I have now turned bearish on the company and its prospects going forward. I had intended to give them the benefit of the doubt, but I see further downside ahead given their less than convincing market penetration to date. Investors and analysts alike will have several opportunities to reassess market demand through product sales and prescription figures, but as it stands, I see them underperforming their own expectations and the general market. From initiating Lenz with a buy, I am now rating them a sell.

data.title: Amazon's Bears Watch FCF Go Negative But Miss CEO's Lion's Share View
data.description:
My Call On Amazon Is Underwater

Amazon (AMZN) has underperformed the market for some time. It is not unusual for this stock to behave in this way, as its upward trajectory is more choppy compared to other steady stocks whose compounding is more predictable. As Q1 2026 winds down, I want to come back to this stock because I think it suffers from short-sighted bearish narratives from time to time. In particular, at the end of a month where investors' attention suddenly shifted from AI-capex fears to the Strait of Hormuz and the Iran war, this article was triggered by the news that cloud infrastructure emerges as the quiet winner of the AI boom as it offered me an opportunity to stress-test my bullish rating that I issued when the stock was at $240 (which means that this call is currently underwater by roughly 13%).

It might be interesting for some who have not followed me since the early innings that my research on Amazon was triggered by two aspects. First of all, I was struck by the big investment Prime Video was making in The Rings of Power Series. As a result, I analyzed the economics of the series, and I had the opportunity to understand the strength and the stickiness of Amazon's ecosystem. Secondly, being a loyal Costco (COST) shareholder, I realized more and more that Amazon was building something similar, but on a larger scale. As a result, I researched, and I did find that Costco truly influenced Amazon Prime. But then I also understood that Amazon's growth is not only about retail sales or Prime subscriptions. It is about its leading role in AWS, it is about possibly the best online ad business on the planet, and it is also about robotics. In one word. This is why I have argued a few times that we need to evolve Amazon's narrative. Moreover, as the AI race unfolds and the hyperscalers rush to build data centers and create the infrastructure needed to support this compute revolution, I have also become quite interested in Amazon's lethal kiss relationship with NVIDIA, as the company is betting big on its in-house-made chips.

One Misleading Figure

Those who are familiar with Amazon will probably skip this part. But I am surprised by how many people are sometimes deceived by Amazon's headline FCF. Take a look at this table, with the financial numbers in $B.

Year Operating Cash Flow CapEx FCF
2023 84.9 52.8 32.1
2024 115.9 83.0 32.9
2025 139.5 132 7.5
2026E 155.0 (est.) 200.0 (guid.) -45.0

We see that Amazon's FCF can swing by almost $80B in just a few years. At the same time, its operating cash flow doesn't do that, but grows steadily. Therefore, the difference depends solely on Amazon's CapEx.

As we see below, when we put together Amazon's operating cash and CapEx, we see that Amazon's operating cash generally more than covers its CapEx.

This year, however, Amazon will likely report negative FCF, which is the first time since 2022, when the company heavily funded its bet in logistics to separate itself from the rest and create a strong infrastructure moat that has been leveraged since then. Look at the operating cash curve from 2023 onward, and we see that the acceleration is brutal.

Investors know that Amazon doesn't truly care about quarterly figures, but invests heavily in bets that can create long-lasting cash flows. The question is whether the AI CapEx will deliver acceptable returns or not.

To address this issue, we have to understand that Amazon truly has five engines that are often not considered.

The 5 Engines For Amazon's Future

AWS

This is easy. We are talking about the largest cloud business on the planet, with FY25 revenue close to $130B, and a growth rate that is once again speeding up above 20% annually (24% as of Q4 2025). I think what CEO Andy Jassy said during the last earnings call went unnoticed, as investors were busy figuring out Amazon's negative FCF and the possible ROIs on its capex. As I have often said, we need to keep in mind that we are talking about infrastructure, and with this perspective, we can position our portfolios correctly to capture the real megatrend. Jassy said that, currently, AI demand is "barbelled", meaning that it is concentrated at two different ends. On one side, a few AI labs and their viral applications (i.e. OpenAI and ChatGPT, Anthropic and Claude); on the other; those companies that are already getting a lot of value out of AI thanks to the productivity dividend we discussed some time ago, mainly because AI can reduce costs (remember when we talked about the benefits for the banks?). Now, Jassy said that the middle part of the barbell is "the largest and the most durable". This is where all other companies sit, and the new businesses that are being invented thanks to AI. This is why the Amazon CEO believes that:

The lion's share of that demand is yet to come in the middle of that barbell. And that will come over time. It will come as you have more and more companies with AI talent, as more and more people get educated with an AI background, as inference continues to get less expensive, and that's a big piece of what we're trying to do with Trainium and our hardware strategy.

I think that every time Jassy speaks, we get some insights into what is unfolding that we rarely find elsewhere. We also learn that Amazon not only owns immense and valuable data, but can also interpret it rather sharply and communicate the essence of what is going on. The metaphor of the barbell is there to prove and I have not seen many analysts or investors concentrate on the middle of the barbell as the place where demand will explode. This means that we could see AWS double or even triple much sooner than we could imagine (my view is that the inflection point will come between 2028 and 2030, pushing AWS revenues above $400B by 2030). With margins above 30%, we would have $120B in operating cash in 2030.

Advertising

Possibly, this is the most overlooked business among the largest ones that Amazon already runs. It is truly a hidden profit-generating machine. In fact, if you think about it, when you are on amazon.com, you already have in mind to buy something. An ad on Amazon is more valuable than an ad on Google or Meta because the conversion rate is higher. Moreover, Amazon has just started to leverage its proprietary database on its members' behaviors to personalize ads on Prime Video. Here, Amazon has a structural advantage due to closed-loop attribution, which Meta and Google don't have to the same degree.

This is already a $68B business, with extremely high operating margins that, even though they are not disclosed, could be around 70%, considering what other peers are reporting. Considering that it is growing at above 20%, we can expect it to be a $170B business in five years, which means that it will generate almost $120B in operating cash. Do you see where I am headed? In a few years, AWS and advertising could generate over $240B in operating cash, which would be more than enough to cover almost any CapEx cycle.

Custom Chips

Let's now get to what many are still not factoring in. Amazon is developing a chip business (ever heard of Graviton and Trainium?). I challenge you to say right away whether Amazon is losing or making money with chips. You might be surprised that, as of Q4 2025, Trainium and Graviton reported a combined revenue run rate of over $10B, with a triple-digit growth rate YoY. These chips are not tools for internal use; they are a product that Amazon can now sell. In a world hungry for chips, you immediately see how Amazon has done everything it could to be ready and not appear unprepared. Moreover, both Trainium and Graviton (Graviton5, in particular) not only generate revenues for AWS, but they also reduce, at the same time, the cost curve. So, the more chips Amazon sells, the wider its margins become: it earns more revenue while its cost structure becomes leaner. No wonder Amazon is already fully booked.

There is also another big advantage. Amazon Bedrock is the inference platform that the company has developed. Its advantage is that it is LLM-agnostic. This gives AWS an advantage because it offers its customers an infrastructure that can run with whatever models the customers want to use. This is why I say that AWS is a utility, and as its infrastructure develops, workloads are taken away from NVIDIA's supply chain.

Robotics

Amazon has deployed over 1M robots in its fulfillment centers, and we know that robots are involved in or assist around 75% of total deliveries worldwide. Guess how Amazon runs its fleet? Yes, through an AI model: DeepFleet. Once again, efficiency has improved, and costs are also down since the introduction of this model.

Here we have the basis for a true financial case that not many consider. We are all paying attention to the timeline of Tesla's Optimus first deliveries (I addressed this, and, as much as I like Tesla's innovations, I have a hard time believing that the company will deliver coherently to what it has promised so far).

We know that Amazon doesn't like to disclose much about its internal KPIs. But a few months ago, a document leak reported by the New York Times showed that between 2025 and 2027, robots were going to deliver almost $13B in savings or $0.30 per shipped item. In Q3 2024, Jassy talked about 25% reduction in fulfillment time in the Shreveport, Louisiana facility and a 25% cost improvement. What was then tested there as a template is now being rolled out to many other fulfillment centers. So far, robots are not yet impacting Amazon's margins, even though we have seen Amazon's retail margins move up a bit. But now that we are reaching a critical mass, we should see true margin benefits in two main ways: retail cost savings and more AWS revenue (robots require AWS to be managed; the more robots, the more workloads for AWS).

Keep in mind that we have some figures: $13B is not a small amount of money. We can expect robotics to have an impact of around $35B through 2030, not counting what Amazon could do if it decides to sell robots to retail or enterprise customers or offer its DeepFleet model to others.

Zoox

Finally, let's get to robotaxis. Everyone looks at Tesla. Waymo has finally gotten some attention. But Zoox is often forgotten. At the same time, it has already delivered 350k free rides as of March 2026. Waymo is at 400k weekly paid rides across six cities in the U.S., but Zoox seems the credible runner-up. Here, Zoox's CEO said that we should not expect this business to generate profits for a few more years. But we should not ignore what this business is about for Amazon. Yes, it is about moving people without a driver, but it is also a key piece of its logistics empire. Think about the cost savings that autonomous driving can generate in metro areas where Amazon delivers thousands of boxes every day. Zoox is about last-mile synergies. On top of that, it will also develop a robotaxi business. When we view it in this way, we can understand why there is extra margin upside that no DCF can currently capture.

Valuation

Everything I said above flows into one funnel, which we then have to evaluate. We can build a DCF, but I think in Amazon's case, we always have to combine it with an SOTP valuation. Moreover, with Amazon, I prefer to consider a 7-year timeframe, rather than 5, due to how its FCF fluctuates.

My estimate is that Amazon's top-line will be above $1T by 2029 and that it could be around $1.33T in 2032. I also assume that its EBITDA margin will gradually move to 28% and that its CapEx will be at around 10% of its revenues. As a result, I expect Amazon to reach at least $100B in FCF by 2029 and to be around $180B by 2032. I am using a discount rate of 9% (my calculation for its WACC) and a terminal growth rate of 4%.

Here are a few scenarios

WACC TGR Peak EBITDA % Share Price
bear 10% 3.5% 23% $148
base 9% 4% 28% $235
bull 8% 4.5% 32% $362

If we assign a probability to each scenario, I will use 35% for the base and the bull scenarios and 30% for the bear one. The probability-weighted average gives us a price of $255.

When we see this, we understand that Amazon is already a bit undervalued considering the base case. But let me show you a sensitivity map that shows how different discount rates and terminal growth rates influence the final share price. We end up with a couple of extremely interesting combinations. For example, if we assume a terminal growth of 4.5%, which, considering that it is a nominal rate with inflation embedded, means that Amazon will only grow at 1.5-2 percentage points above inflation, we see that Amazon could be valued between $278 and $422, depending on the WACC we use (in any case, 8% is not truly low).

Let's use the sum-of-the-parts method for a quick check. I estimate for AWS $49B in EBIT, which I value at a 22x multiple to get an EV of $1.08B. The advertising business does almost $70B in revenue. At a 12x EV/revenue multiple, we have $840B in EV. The retail business could be valued at 1x EV/revenue, which means that we have a value of roughly $520B. For the chips business, I am using an 8x EV/revenue multiple, which means it is worth $80B. Zoox currently doesn't make any money. But I think we could easily say that it is worth around $50B, considering what Tesla is worth, how Waymo is doing, and the strategic value that Zoox would have on Amazon's internal operations. The total enterprise value would be $2.57T, subtracting the net debt of $55B, we have an equity value of $2.52B. Divide this by the 10.7B shares, and we have a share price of $235, which suggests that Amazon is still undervalued. So, the catalyst here clearly becomes the next FCF pivot, when Amazon will prove how its FCF generation power has significantly increased.

Two Strategies While We Wait

After taking some time to study options, I have recently started using them. In Amazon's case, there are two ETFs that could be considered in the current situation. I don't expect Amazon to surge rapidly this year. It is more likely that the stock will either trade within the $180-$220 range, or that it will just go down one quarterly report after the other as investors see the company report negative FCF. In this environment, we can hold Amazon for the long term, but we could expose ourselves to either the Kurv Yield Premium Strategy Amazon ETF (AMZP) or the YieldMax AMZN Option Income Strategy ETF AMZY.

AMZP holds AMZN shares and writes calls at a higher strike. So it does offer us some upside, which is obviously capped at the strike price. But its NAV is linked to AMZN equity and often offers less share price erosion. In the meantime, I think we are in an environment where covered calls can play a meaningful role. AMZN is a great business, but the stock is currently stuck and has had a hard time moving up. Until its CapEx normalizes, AMZP lets us collect yield and even profit if the stock moves up a bit. It is a way to monetize the period we spend holding our Amazon shares without giving them up. And when I say monetizing, I mean that it yields above 20%. The risk is capped upside, while being fully exposed to AMZN's drops.

AMZY, on the other hand, doesn't hold AMZN shares because it uses a synthetic covered call with Treasuries as collateral. It builds its exposure via FLEX options (flexible exchange options, which allow the two counterparties to negotiate terms such as expiry date, strike price, and terms of settlement). It pays a weekly dividend, which currently yields above 50%. But this has a cost: if AMZN moves down, so does AMZY; when AMZN moves up quickly. The goal here is to have weekly income. Currently, the yield is high because volatility on AMZN is high. Volatility means higher option premium that is collected and distributed. If we expect AMZN to trade in a range for some time, as I am, this is a very interesting way to monetize our patience. The risk is capital loss if AMZN drops and NAV erosion when AMZN moves up sharply in a short time. Moreover, this is not a way to own AMZN, but a way to simply cash on Amazon's current volatility, which makes sense if the stock moves sideways.

Conclusion

I am long Amazon and the more I look at it, the more I am convinced the stock is already in value territory, with very mild expectations baked into today's price. Patience has a cost, but there are ways to monetize it through options or by simply increasing the stake in Amazon as its share price is currently below its fair value. Five years from now, I think the chances of being in the red with my positions are extremely low, and this is why I consider Amazon one of the best companies I have a stake in.

data.title: Alphabet: The Social Media Lawsuit Is Not A Long-Term Risk
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Over the last month, as the overall market has sold off as a result of the war in Iran, Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) shares have dropped 21% since their peak in February. Technically, this would mean that shares are in a bear market.

At the heart of Alphabet’s selloff is the recent finding by a jury in Los Angeles which found that the company’s video streaming platform (YouTube) was liable for promoting social media addiction that caused harm on the Plaintiff (K.G.M or Kaley).

While the market is worrying this could set off a cascading event leading to Alphabet being dragged into more lawsuits, I think this lawsuit will end up being a bump in the road in hindsight.

In essence, I believe this lawsuit is a distraction from Alphabet’s real business lines and durable growth. For investors who want to use Warren Buffett's famous line “be greedy when others are fearful” I believe now could be a good time to take advantage of the dip in share price.

With this, I continue to believe shares of the California-based tech giant are still a Strong Buy. This lawsuit appears concerning on the surface. But I believe that buried in the details of this lawsuit the facts show this will have limited long-term effects on the business.

Why I’m Doing Follow-Up Coverage

When I last wrote on Alphabet earlier this year, I was more focused on the tech giants rapid advancements in quantum computing. I continue to believe this is one of the most important projects the company is working on, and I believe this lawsuit is noise.

But, as Warren Buffett’s mentor Benjamin Graham has said “in the short run the market is a voting machine.” In this case, the market is voting that the outcome of this lawsuit in LA is far more pressing to the long term value of Alphabet’s stock price than the huge developments they have made in the quantum sphere.

In a few words, I disagree. With this, I am doing follow-up coverage to show how this lawsuit is not a long-term risk and that the real opportunities for Alphabet’s upside just got better.

LA Lawsuit Is Not A Risk

On Wednesday, a Jury in California awarded the Plaintiff, Kaley, a total of $6 million in damages as a result of a lawsuit against major social media giants YouTube (part of Alphabet) and Facebook’s owner, Meta Platforms (META).

The markets are not selling off due to the total of $6 million in damages. The markets don’t like this outcome because some participants believe this could open the flood gate to more lawsuits/changing regulations that could put these tech giants in a bad position.

More specifically, some experts are stating:

...[This is] the social media industry’s “Big Tobacco” moment, comparing it with the 1990s, when tobacco companies were forced to pay billions of dollars for lying to the public about the safety and potential harms of their products.

Respectfully, I disagree. To understand how big this could be, we have to look at the fact pattern for this lawsuit.

In this case, the Plaintiff sued the tech giants alleging that the platforms caused her to become addicted to them which exasperated her mental health condition. According to reports:

The 20-year-old woman alleged that she suffered from severe body dysmorphia, depression and suicidal thoughts due to her near-constant use of the apps and the constant notifications that made it difficult for her to stop.

While I sympathize with the Plaintiff and admit social media can clearly be addictive, I am concerned that many similar lawsuits that could arise will be able to demonstrate this fact pattern. Simply being addicted to social media will not entitle you to compensation. The fact pattern will likely have to be similar to this where there is objective harm. This is where I believe most copycat lawsuits will fall apart.

Here in the U.S., to be able to be successful in most civil suits, you have to be able to quantify or explicitly qualify damages the defendant caused you to incur. For many cases of social media addiction, this will be difficult.

Overall, I expect this to be either a one-off or a limited slate of lawsuits by users who are addicted. I will talk more about this in the risks section, but Alphabet does have some federal laws that protect them as well.

Valuation

While Alphabet’s shares have come under pressure over the past two months since I last wrote on them, I believe the valuation story has only gotten better.

If we look at the company’s forward EPS estimates, growth still appears to be strong, with the median Wall Street analyst expecting EPS growth to clock in at 7.04% YoY compared to 2025 EPS numbers.

With this, if we look at overall EPS revisions for 2026, this still appears to be net up compared to what estimates were both 1, 3, and 6 months ago. While some investors are taking this jury outcome and using it as a sell the news event, Wall Street is only getting more bullish from here.

On some equities, I believe institutional investors are wrong. For a big tech company (like Alphabet) I think the smart money is right here. I believe the company’s story will get better going forward. I don’t believe this will become a cascading legal issue.

With EPS growth looking stable, the valuation looks really attractive at these prices. While the company’s forward P/E of 24.28 is 83.4% higher than the sector median, the company’s forward revenue growth is over 236% higher than the sector median. In essence, you are paying a 83.4% premium valuation to unlock over 236% top line growth. I think this still makes sense.

With this, I think shares should be trading at a 100% premium to the sector median forward P/E to account for the fact that the company will (most likely) still continue to book strong growth. This lawsuit will not be a long-term catastrophic risk to the company like the market has been implying with the share price moves over the last 8 weeks.

With this, if we saw the premium expand to 100% from 83.4%, this would represent 19.90% upside from here.

Risks

I believe the biggest risk of this lawsuit is not the effective "fine" the lawsuit has assessed against Alphabet. Rather, as I touched on before, I believe the biggest risk is that this could cascade into more lawsuits against the company nationwide. As Reuters reported:

The Los Angeles trial is meant to serve as a bellwether, or test case, for the thousands of similar lawsuits consolidated in California state courts.

To be clear, and as NPR outlined in their reporting on this lawsuit earlier this week, the verdict by the jury in California is "rare." With it being rare, I expect Alphabet (and Meta for their part) to appeal their respective outcomes of this lawsuit.

At the federal level, the ace in the hole for these companies is Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act of 1996 which protects the major social media platforms (including Alphabet’s YouTube) from being held liable for content that was posted by users on their platform. I believe this is the silver lining.

For Alphabet, the story is straightforward: appeal this ruling (they have already indicated they will) and lean on Section 230 to remove liability from their services as a platform. In essence, the heart of this caselaw going forward will likely be whether the platforms themselves are addictive (and therefore cause harm) or if it's the content itself that is posted on the platform that is addictive.

After all, if platforms like YouTube had no user generated content on them, their value to a consumer would be minimal. I believe Alphabet will lean hard on this during the appeal process which will likely be in front of a series of judges, not a jury. Judges often rule differently on appeal than a Jury may have originally. This is because judges here in the U.S. will most often assess a case on appeal on the basis that the law was applied incorrectly. I think Alphabet via YouTube has a solid case here.

Bottom Line

While Alphabet losing this lawsuit and being ordered to pay $1.5 million + 30% of a remaining $3 million joint judgement between them and Meta is clearly concerning, I am not worried about the overall implications to the company in the long run like the market appears to be.

Alphabet’s business is incredibly diversified with roughly 15% of revenues coming from its YouTube division, which is the part of the company that was targeted in this lawsuit.

Even if this lawsuit led to later lawsuits that somehow cut YouTube’s revenue by 33%, the $20 billion in lost topline revenue could likely be replaced by growth from other divisions of the company. Keep in mind that the company grew 15% YoY on over $400 billion in topline revenue. Even growth that is ½ this pace (7.5% YoY topline growth) would more than offset a 33% drop in YouTube’s revenue due to changes in how they can place ads/push engagement time with users going forward.

In essence, Alphabet’s business is incredibly durable. The market (especially in the last month) has been pricing their business like its brittle. I disagree.

With this, and despite the selloff, shares remain a Strong Buy.

data.title: MNTN: Surprise Product-Led Growth Could Unlock Multiple Expansion
data.description:
MNTN (MNTN) is an ad-tech company based in the US that focuses on performance-based connected TV (CTV) advertising. The platform allows brands to run automated advertising campaigns on TV and measure the ad performance. Having covered a few companies in the ad-tech sector, I found the CTV market to be quite attractive due to the overall shift from linear to digital programming, with the competitive landscape typically segmented by the type of clients being served.

For instance, since MNTN offers its solutions to brands or advertisers that demand advertising slots, then its business is categorized as a DSP (Demand Side Platform). In that sense, it competes with the likes of Trade Desk (TTD) or Vibe. On the other hand, there are also companies offering an SSP (Supply Side Platform) solution on the opposite side to media companies owning the advertising slots, which, in this context, is irrelevant to MNTN’s competitive landscape despite being in the same industry.

Since its May 2025 IPO at $16 per share, overall performance has been underwhelming, with the stock currently trading merely at $8.6 per share, losing half of its IPO valuation. The stock is also down almost -30% YTD, suggesting a relatively cautious market sentiment on the stock as of late.

I initiate my coverage with a buy rating. My 1-year price target of $9.52 suggests that MNTN appears undervalued.

Financial Reviews

Overall, it seems that fundamentals are quite decent and improving. In Q4, MNTN continues to realize double-digit growth as it benefits from the ad budgets shift towards the CTV platform. In Q4 2025, MNTN delivered a revenue of $87.1 million, a 36% YoY growth. For FY 2025, MNTN realized almost 29% growth and a FY revenue of $290 million.

The key highlight in Q4, I believe, is in the bottom-line performance for the FY, which sees an improvement across the board. Overall, this seems to be driven by the strong revenue growth and also the company’s ability to manage the expansion of its cost of revenues. In FY 2025, operating income turned positive to $24 million as a result, displaying a significant swing from last year’s loss.

Meanwhile, adjusted EBITDA (aEBITDA) for the FY reached almost $68 million, reflecting a 23% margin, an expansion from 17.2% the prior year.

Liquidity and cash flow generation are quite solid, in my view, as MNTN finished the FY with almost $210 million of cash and equivalents with zero debts, with the company generating over $56 million of operating cash flow (OCF) for the FY, a better figure compared to last year’s and a stronger position compared to its IPO levels.

Catalyst

I think the catalyst will remain moderate to strong into 2026. Supported by the secular industry shift into CTV digital ads, I believe MNTN’s offerings should continue to be in demand. However, it is also important to give credits to how well MNTN may capture such an opportunity. In that sense, I may project decent growth in 2026 from the potential adoption of its newly-released AI-driven platform QuickFrame.

In short, the platform helps simplify video content generation for SMBs, MNTN’s target market, in a way it could reduce production cost for TV advertising. To a greater extent, I view this as a way for MNTN to possibly capture some market share from its competitor TDD, which focuses on serving larger enterprise teams with bigger advertising budgets. With the platform gaining 5,000 users since its beta launch, as commented by an analyst in the earnings call, the growth prospect appears to be quite promising:

I want to ask about more details, Mark, on just QuickFrame AI. With the adoption ramping here, I think I heard 5,000 users. Would love to hear any insights you have on improvements to the business as a result of it in terms of time-to-market perhaps maybe post-sales cycle?

Source: Q4 earnings call.

Another catalyst that is worth mentioning here is the integration with Magnite (MGNI), an ad-tech company I also recently covered. Since MGNI is an SSP, the integration makes a lot of sense, as it should unlock premium live-streaming ad inventories for MNTN’s existing advertiser users, potentially driving higher conversion rates and larger advertisers into the platform. As expected, the management also was equally optimistic on this prospect as per their comment in earnings call:

Yes. Absolutely. I mean, we're very bullish on that partnership. We have a really good relationship with Magnite. And specifically, when we announced it, we were talking about paused ads, but there's also live sports and other very premium content that we've introduced both through our direct partnerships with the media companies, all of whom we have direct partnerships with, but also through Magnite.

Source: Q4 earnings call.

Risk

Overall, I would say that risk remains minimal to moderate for MNTN. While the escalating geopolitical tension may pose a risk, for instance, through rising energy cost concerns that could make SMBs cut advertising budgets, it is probably too early to grade these risk factors as immediate. Nonetheless, the fact that I mentioned them here means that I am not entirely disregarding this risk factor.

On the other hand, the intensifying competition in the CTV space may present a higher risk factor, in my view, especially from the likes of Meta (META) or TTD, both larger players that could at some point enter MNTN’s space with their SMB-focused solutions that could threaten MNTN’s market share.

Valuation/Pricing

My target price for MNTN is driven by the following assumptions for the bull vs bear scenarios of the FY 2026 projection, based on the consensus analysis:

  1. Bull scenario (50% probability) assumptions - I assume MNTN exceeds the high-end of the consensus estimate, with the QuickFrame adoption and the MGNI partnership contributing significantly to the overperformance. I would expect MNTN to see a re-rating of its P/S to 2.5x, surpassing today’s level as the market assigns a premium valuation to the stock.

  2. Bear scenario (50% probability) assumptions - I expect a relatively muted sentiment in the ad-tech market, due to concerns on ad spending cut amid longer-than-anticipated geopolitical tension. I assign MNTN a P/S multiple of 1.5x, which aligns with its TTM P/S today.

Consolidating all the information above into my model, I arrived at an FY 2026 weighted target price of $9.5 per share, implying around 15% upside. At this point, I give MNTN a buy rating. To account for the risk factor and be a bit conservative in my projection, I give each scenario a 50% probability.

Conclusion

Since IPO, MNTN has successfully delivered decent operating results despite underwhelming share performance. Today, it remains a profitable ad-tech leader focusing on CTV, a segment I believe will continue to grow largely due to secular shifts in TV viewership. I expect decent revenue growth to continue, with the possibility of a surprise if its partnership with another CTV-focused player on the SSP side, MGNI, turns out to be successful. As such, execution remains key despite minimal foreseeable risk today. I believe my projection, while assuming a potential overperformance in the bull scenario, remains conservative since I also assign a 50-50 probability despite minimal risk. My price target analysis suggests that MNTN appears undervalued today.

data.title: A Perspective On Why The Oil Price Is More Likely To Reach $150 Than $50
data.description:
The essence of this is simple enough. Currently, the world is missing somewhere between 10 and 20 million barrels of oil per day. This is equivalent to 10-20% of the global oil supply. Of the global energy base, oil makes up roughly 30%, meaning that 6% of global energy is currently missing. There is no GDP without energy, no industrial activity. The most recent examples of energy shocks of this magnitude are the 1973 and 1979 oil crises, where 6-7% of the global oil supply was disrupted, making this a much larger disruption. We simply have no precedent to compare with.

Why do I say between 10% and 20% when it’s documented that 20% of global oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz? I do so because a few alternatives in the form of inland pipelines exist through Saudi Arabia, but they don’t have sufficient flow rate to compensate for the combined sum of vessels passing through the strait on a daily basis up until the ongoing war started.

“Soon,” I don’t know when, you don’t know when, no one knows when—but “soon” national and local inventories will dry up as the last tankers that went through the strait will, in most instances, have arrived at their destination. At the same time, the global strategic 400 million barrel reserve will provide a buffer, but perhaps not equally. When that happens, the global oil market will have to find a price equilibrium equivalent to the destruction of 10-20% of daily oil consumption.

What will that pricing point be? Again, you don’t know, I don’t know, no one knows, but it will be a lot higher than the price today. Why? Because developed economies will be able to pay a premium in order to prioritize their economic activity, and this will be all about the marginal price at that point. We saw the same thing during the 2022 European gas crisis, where the EU’s ability to pay a premium drove up prices.

In terms of who will be hit the hardest, it’s well documented that the majority of oil and gas passing through the strait reaches Asian economies in particular, but also the European market. The oil market is a global market, so the Americas will also feel the impact, but on a different scale. Having said that, however, stocks won’t differentiate, as this is a global economy, so here, we’re all at risk.

An Abundance Of Dilemmas

If you haven’t already picked up on it, I belong to the nervous part of the crowd observing the current situation unfold. This will show itself in how I view the situation throughout the coming sections, giving reflections on different aspects of the situation.

Some dilemmas fade, and others flare up on a daily basis, but here are some of the ones that I find relevant at the current time.

  • Iran is in no hurry to end the conflict, as they are incentivized to ensure this is as costly as possible to act as a deterrent from ending up in a similar situation 6-12 months down the road. Simply put, they were on the receiving end of a 12-day conflict last summer, and the U.S./Israel started bombing them mid-negotiations in February. They need to ensure this bites back hard on the U.S./Israel to void future attacks.
  • U.S. and Israel goals for ending the conflict may not be aligned, meaning Israel could keep the conflict warm after the U.S. wants to end it. Meaning the strait remains closed.
  • Respective negotiation positions are further apart now than before the disagreement escalated into war, suggesting a limited likelihood that a sudden solution is found.
  • Will the strait automatically reopen if the U.S. declares “mission accomplished” and goes home, or would the first point above incentivize Iran to keep it closed until they achieved favorable terms to ensure the conflict doesn’t reappear 6-12 months from now?
  • The U.S. is potentially about to launch a ground-based invasion with the purpose of controlling hundreds of kilometers of coastal area with the purpose of establishing a buffer zone to reopen the strait. As the U.S. has no interest in a “forever” war, what then happens when they depart the area again?

Background & Context

The conflicts and dilemmas evolve daily, so just a few notes on current status.

Below you can see an illustration of the complete halt of maritime traffic taking place in the strait.

Official data above should be observed with respect to GPS signals being turned off, etc., so should be observed with caution despite showing a directionally very clear picture.

The strait is effectively closed due to the credible threat of Iranian strikes on tankers through primarily boat-based operations, as Iran has limited possibility of missile and drone strikes due to them being launched with fixed GPS target positions against a moving target.

This threat has proven credible, as Iran successfully has managed to attack and damage a number of ships, no less than twenty-four so far into the conflict. In fact, Iran only needs to strike a few with regular occurrence to keep the threat credible, causing insurers to cancel policies, prompting maritime traffic to stand still. No vessel operator wants to run the risk of causing a massive oil spill or losing cargo worth hundreds of millions of dollars.

Pricing Development That Illustrates Current Predicament

Depending on whether you take WTI (CL1:COM) or Brent (CO1:COM), the price is a bit different, but both appear to have stabilized at their current levels. WTI has settled at levels below $100 per barrel, while Brent has settled at levels above $100 per barrel.

These are extremely liquid and highly traded commodities, and the trading exceeds the physical trade underneath. As such, a disconnect can appear at the surface, but if we observe jet fuel, heating oil, and other commodities from the original barrel of oil, we observe much different price movements.

Above you can observe the price movements in jet fuel, which has spiked drastically as it’s a less traded commodity, where we can better observe the physical reality.

Above we see the similar pricing development for heating oil—same point, that physical reality is better depicted. These are the little signs where prices are much higher than what $100 per barrel of oil would indicate.

Coming back to oil, it’s my firm belief, as stated earlier, that at one point, the traded price will have to reflect physical reality. Right now, the market is forecasting an end to the conflict in the short- to mid-term. No prolonged conflict, no upward rip in inflation. I’m of the belief that the market is currently misinterpreting the situation.

Fundamental realities haven’t changed just because President Trump communicated productive conversations with Iran, but expectations do, and therefore, we’re still at $100 per barrel, which helps explain the disconnect. Market perception is that the US will declare mission accomplished as it doesn’t have the staying power, similarly to the tariffs and Greenland situation, and then all this goes away.

During the 1973 oil embargo, WTI increased 300% in price, and yes, it lasted longer than the duration of the current crisis. It’s just to illustrate the point that at one point, the trading reality will have to match the physical reality of being 10-20 million barrels of oil short in the global economy on a daily basis, and I believe the physical tightness shown in these other examples is relevant to consider.

If this goes on for weeks longer, we need physical reality to catch up. We’re already now seeing countries introduce limits related to purchasing, for instance, diesel. Thailand, Malaysia, and Pakistan are countries with very limited oil reserves, causing governments to intervene to try and limit societal activity that consumes oil. Less driving, idle flights, etc. United Airlines has now communicated the need to idle upwards of 5% of their flights to limit the impact of an oil price above $100. If this goes on, we’re only seeing the beginning of initiatives such as these.

There is no short-term fix. You can’t make a jet plane go by electricity, you can’t make your combustion engine car drive on electricity, and so it goes.

Major oil shocks have historically been significant catalysts for global recessions, and I see this no different should it continue for a little while longer. Having to take 10-20 million barrels out of the equation represents a pricing point that can push the global economy into recession. Already now, consumers are starting to feel the economic hardship and pain, which will result in worsening consumer sentiments eroding demand, and so the negative business cycle starts.

I referenced the 2022 European gas crisis at the early onset of this article, and I want to add an additional point to that perspective. If the oil price needs to go much higher due to developed nations and economies being able to absorb higher prices for gas, for instance, it will have a ripple effect in driving up the price of its substitute, coal, which would impact poorer countries negatively. This would put enormous economic strain on economies already struggling.

The knock-on effects are also worth reflecting upon, as more than 500,000 containers enter the region through the strait on a monthly basis, with upwards of 250,000 going the other direction. Supply of consumer staples has effectively halted.

Unwrapping Some Of The Dilemma's & Asymmetry In Military Might

Just to recap the prime dilemmas as I consider

  • Iran is in no hurry to end the conflict.
  • Misalignment between the U.S. / Israel for when conditions are met for ending the conflict.
  • Respective positioning between the parties further apart than before conflict erupted into war.
  • Would the strait even reopen if the aggressors declared mission accomplished and halted offensive operations?
  • With a potential ground-based invasion, what’s the long-term solution without having to concede very unfavorable conditions towards Iran.

The U.S. & Israel have effectively carpet-bombed Iran for the past three-plus weeks, and still, Iran maintains the capability to strike back at its enemies and their infrastructure throughout the region. At a very limited scale, albeit, but it remains able to do so.

With Iranian infrastructure crumbling as every day goes by and being massively overmatched in military capabilities, why do I then insist that Iran has little incentive to seek an end to the conflict?

Iran was attacked last summer during the 12-year war, and now again, with the loss of several high-ranking officials as the consequence, posing a real threat to the regime in power. In order to avoid ending up on the short end of the military stick once again, the regime needs to make this as costly as possible for the U.S. (and the world) to ensure there’ll be no third strike on Iran again, possibly ever. The way to ensure that is to keep the global economy hostage for as long as it’s at all possible for Iran.

The people haven’t revolted despite the intense bombing, and there is little reason to believe the regime is about to lose its grip on the country. As such, it’ll remain in place once this campaign is over, reinforcing the need to ensure the point raised before.

I would expect the Iranian regime won’t accept an end to the conflict before very favorable terms are presented.
