The alternative is a full-scale invasion with all that it takes, and that’s not feasible with the current mobilization in the area in combination with the political downside at home, where the war is quite unpopular across both sides of the aisle.

Then comes the potential misalignment between U.S. and Israeli military objectives. It’s been hard to fully understand what the U.S. goal has been, as various things have been communicated. However, for Israel, this is a long-standing enemy who has posed threats in the direction of total destruction of Israel, so this is their one chance to go at it. As such, I believe Israel is willing to see this conflict last longer in order to cripple the regime fully. What I’m suggesting here is that even if the U.S. pulled out, Israel might not be ready to stop, then of course, the U.S. might force Israel to do so due to ending munitions supply or otherwise.

The last point I want to touch upon is the negotiation stance. It’s difficult for anybody to fully understand the situation, as the U.S. administration gives daily updates of progress and the conflict being weeks, not months, away from ending. The opposite side of that coin is Iran denying any peace talks. President Trump communicated that Iran had been presented with a 15-point ceasefire plan, to which Iran responded with a list of five conditions that had to be met. This included ensuring the war will not recur, payment of war damages and reparations, as well as Iranian sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz.

Negotiations often start with the two parties being far apart, but as both David and Goliath consider themselves in an advantageous position currently, it could take a long while, if even at all, to reach a mutual understanding. Raising the question of whether the U.S. can bomb Iran into submission before the impact on the global economy gets too large and past the breaking point.

This is a conflict where one party is substantially stronger than the other, which has been the case in most conflicts between the US and other states since the second world war. The difference is that Iran has the possibility of inflicting strong direct and indirect damage on the global economy, despite being much weaker, giving them an edge prior opponents didn’t have.

This is about risk perception, and it doesn’t take more than a dinghy boat with explosives to maintain that threat despite being up against the most powerful military on the globe. It’s not about closing the strait; it’s about presenting and maintaining the threat of being able to inflict damage on traffic passing through.

I think Iran was underestimated and that initiative was lost somewhere during the conflict. Iran, with respect to its limited position of power, took some smart decisions, such as dispersing its military decision-making capability while engaging all neighboring states, which at first didn’t appear smart but ensures it’s easier for them to trade blows of similar magnitude in a tit-for-tat exchange under threat of mutual destruction via attacks on energy infrastructure that takes years to repair and makes up a substantial part of the global oil and gas supply.

Arguments Suggesting I'm Wrong

First of all, the U.S. administration keeps communicating that they intend to stop their campaign within weeks, not months. If that’s the case, balance should be able to return without permanent damage to the global economy to an extent where it results in a recession.

Secondly, other major economies and allies have a strong interest in seeing this come to an end. Japan and South Korea, household U.S. allies, are very reliant on the products flowing through the strait, and I would expect they are already leaning hard on the U.S. to wrap it up and go home. Similarly, while not an ally, China would also have a very strong similar interest, as they have no oil production. It’s well known they carry massive reserves, but that doesn’t mean they won’t try and influence the situation. Pakistan, Thailand, Malaysia, and all other major developing Asian nations will want this to end, as energy makes up a significant part of any developing nation's GDP, indicating vulnerability to what’s ongoing.

High oil prices are bad for consumers, and this administration cares about the price at the pump. While the current situation would have impacted the U.S. economy harder in earlier decades, today it’s mostly a distributional problem, meaning it will impact consumers more than the broad economy, and that is something President Trump supposedly wouldn’t be willing to tolerate for an extended period.

So the list goes; there are, of course, arguments in both camps, but the above-mentioned are some of the more likely as to why the conflict would soon come to an end, meaning this article would age poorly.

Investing In The Current Environment

Rarely has there been a better time to deploy the famous Peter Lynch quote.

“Far more money has been lost by investors preparing for corrections, or trying to anticipate corrections, than has been lost in corrections themselves.”

It’s a dilemma, because if the conflict is resolved tomorrow, likely the market would bounce back. If the conflict persists, this could be a bear market scenario not too far down the road. As always, no one knows.

One thing we do know is that energy and defense stocks have appreciated, with most other sectors going in the opposite direction. I don’t think it’s smart to invest in energy and defense, as they are exposed to downside risk at this point from the strong appreciation. Similarly, with the limited visibility of whether this persists weeks or months, it’s not that appetizing picking up stocks that are down 10% as a result of the market volatility over the past month.

In fact, I’d much rather stay on the sidelines until we have better clarity. I have a balanced portfolio of both high- and low-beta stocks, but if I had a portfolio with a higher beta (more volatile) than the broader market, I’d consider the opportunity for taking the top off, take some profits, and await the outcome.

Conclusion

If you made it this far, it’s fairly obvious that I’m concerned. Seen from my chair, this is at a point where it’s close to being out of control. No party feels forced to pull back, and it’s a difficult military objective to ensure the threat towards the strait is neutralized. The Iranian regime is pushed up into a corner, desperate and with nowhere to go but fight as best it can. There is no easy way to end this conflict and threat currently impacting the global oil and gas market, not to mention we didn’t discuss food supply, fertilizer, etc.

This isn’t a market where I want to add to my portfolio, so I’ll grow my cash base and even consider if there are positions in my portfolio that I find to be overvalued, where I would then reduce a bit, as cash feels like the safer option now, even if it means risking a sudden upwards market tear should President Trump communicate tomorrow that the conflict has ended.

data.title: IGF: A Guide To The iShares Global Infrastructure ETF
data.description:
The iShares Global Infrastructure ETF (IGF) is a passively managed fund that invests in large infrastructure companies around the world. This educational overview covers IGF’s strategy, what’s in the portfolio, historical performance, how it has responded in different markets, and how it compares to other global infrastructure ETFs.

How's IGF Structured?

IGF tracks the S&P Global Infrastructure Index, which includes 75 stocks. Eligibility: trade on a major exchange, float-adjusted market cap of $100+ million, average daily volume of at least $500,000.

The index is split into three clusters: transportation, utilities, and energy infrastructure. Up to 15 slots are reserved for emerging-market stocks to ensure they aren’t crowded out by larger names. Each cluster is assigned a target weight: roughly 40% each for transportation and utilities, 20% for energy. No single stock can exceed 5% of the index. The index is rebalanced quarterly.

IGF launched in December 2007 and has ~$10 billion in net assets. The expense ratio is 0.39%.

What's In The iShares Global Infrastructure ETF?

IGF currently holds 76 positions, which is actually one more than the index’s target. The top ten make up ~38% of assets. NextEra Energy (NEE) is the top holding, followed by Spanish airport operator Aena (ANNSF) and Australia’s Transurban Group (TRAUF).

Holdings across the portfolio include regulated electric utilities, toll road and railroad operators, pipeline companies, and airport concessionaires.

The current allocation across the three clusters is shown above. Again, these weights are set by the index methodology, so they don’t drift much between rebalances. Turnover is 14%.

The U.S. makes up about 40% of assets, with the rest being international. Australia, Spain, and Canada each comprise about 8-9%, followed by Mexico, France, and China.

As of March 2026, the overall P/E was 20x with a P/B of 2.5x.

IGF: Income Profile And Taxes

IGF pays distributions semi-annually, in June and December. The 30-day SEC yield is around 2.7%, with a slightly higher trailing 12-month yield. That may be lower than expected from a utility- or energy-heavy portfolio. That’s because the transportation cluster (airports, toll roads, etc.) includes companies that pay small dividends or none at all.

IGF’s dividends are mostly qualified, taxed at the lower long-term capital gains rates. This works well in a brokerage account. But there’s an additional wrinkle with the international holdings. Foreign governments withhold taxes on dividends paid by companies domiciled in their countries. In a U.S. taxable account, IGF passes through foreign tax credits that investors can claim, partially offsetting that withholding. In a Roth, however, there’s no U.S. tax liability to offset, so the foreign withholding is effectively lost. For a fund where roughly 60% of holdings are outside the U.S., that’s not trivial. A traditional IRA or taxable account preserves that benefit.

How Might IGF Fit In A Portfolio?

If you want similar infrastructure exposure, you could try to build your own basket. Utilities is the easy one—the Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU) covers that. But IGF’s transportation cluster (toll road concessionaires, airport operators, etc.) doesn't have a clean domestic ETF equivalent. The energy infrastructure piece is mostly midstream pipeline companies. You could cover this through an MLP-focused fund, but taxes would be treated differently. IGF’s value proposition is bundling all three with the international exposure domestic funds don’t provide.

The fund’s recent beta vs. the S&P 500 was around 0.57. Translation: IGF has moved about half as much as the broader market in either direction. Low correlation can be good for diversifying equity-heavy portfolios or reducing overall volatility.

IGF’s returns also depend on movements of the U.S. dollar against the euro, Australian dollar, Canadian dollar, etc. When USD weakens, foreign holdings are “worth more,” and vice versa. For investors whose portfolios are concentrated in U.S. dollar assets, funds like IGF add another layer of diversification. Of course that can also work against you in periods of dollar strength.

How Has The iShares Global Infrastructure ETF Performed?

IGF’s track record is long but uneven. For the first dozen years or so after its 2007 launch, the fund was roughly flat on a total return basis. Dividends offset price declines but didn’t produce much in the way of cumulative gains.

That changed after the pandemic, though, with IGF making gains in four of the past five calendar years. Annualized total returns have been about 14% over the past three years and 10% over five. Since inception, the annualized figure is 4.5%.

IGF has closely tracked its benchmark, which isn’t surprising for a passively managed index fund with a low expense ratio. It has also slightly outperformed the Morningstar category average on a risk-adjusted basis.

How IGF Might Act In Different Markets?

Since ~40% of IGF is in utilities, interest rates are a big factor. When rates fall, bonds and savings accounts pay less, and investors tend to bid up stocks with steady dividends. 2024-2025 was a great example of how rate-cut expectations can boost the fund. But when rates rise, the reverse likely happens: higher-yielding alternatives compete for the same capital.

Transportation holdings (toll roads, airports, etc.) respond to traffic. These types of companies often have contracts tied to inflation, so rising prices isn’t a bad thing. A lasting slowdown in travel (e.g., due to recession) would pressure this part of the portfolio, though.

Energy infrastructure is a different story. Pipeline operators care more about how much oil and gas flows through their systems than commodity prices. Throughput is stable under long-term contracts.

The most important thing to understand here is that these three clusters don’t respond to the same forces at the same time. Utilities are rate-sensitive, transportation is tied to economic activity, and energy infrastructure follows volume and contracts. The underlying businesses keep collecting tolls, transmitting electricity, and moving fuel regardless of what equity markets are doing. That’s part of why IGF has had relatively shallow drawdowns during broad selloffs. The tradeoff is lagging during momentum-led rallies.

How Does IGF Compare To Peers?

I found three other ETFs with similar global infrastructure exposure. I’ve ordered them from most to least similar.

The State Street SPDR S&P Global Infrastructure ETF (GII) tracks the same index as IGF. Their returns have been nearly identical, with small deviations due to the fee difference (0.40% vs. 0.39%) and how each fund handles dividend reinvestments, cash management, and rebalance execution. The main difference is size. GII has ~$840 million in assets vs. IGF’s ~$10 billion, and its daily trading volume (~50K shares) is a fraction of IGF’s (~975K). That can matter for those who care about liquidity and tight bid-ask spreads.

The ProShares DJ Brookfield Global Infrastructure ETF (TOLZ) tracks a different index which requires companies to earn at least 70% of their cash flows from infrastructure operations. IGF’s index picks stocks based on industry classification; TOLZ’s applies a stricter revenue test. The resulting portfolio leans more heavily into utilities and energy infrastructure—about 77% currently vs. the 62% for IGF. Transportation exposure is much smaller. Speaking of small, TOLZ has just ~$180 million in assets, so liquidity could be a concern.

The FlexShares STOXX Global Broad Infrastructure Index Fund (NFRA) casts a wider net, covering energy, transportation, utilities, communications, and government outsourcing. The latter category includes hospital operators, prisons, and postal services under government contracts. Communications stocks like Verizon (VZ) and Deutsche Telekom (DTEGY) make up nearly a quarter of NFRA’s portfolio, which is quite different from IGF.

Summary

The iShares Global Infrastructure ETF is a passive, index-based fund. It invests in around 75 large infrastructure companies across developed and emerging markets. The portfolio is split roughly 40/40/20 between utilities, transportation, and energy infrastructure. Returns tend to come mostly via regulated cash flows, usage-based revenues, and dividends. That gives the fund a defensive profile.

This article answers four key questions about IGF:

  • What is IGF’s strategy, and what does the portfolio hold?
  • How has IGF performed since its inception?
  • How might IGF respond to different market environments?
  • How does IGF compare with other global infrastructure ETFs?

Author’s Note: This analysis reflects data and information available as of March 2026.

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Ondas: From Drone Niche To Defense Platform (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Back in November last year, I was bullish on Ondas Inc. (ONDS) after the company reported strong Q3 results. I am now even more bullish after the Q4 earnings release, which I consider to be an inflection point in how the market perceives this company.

The highlight in Q4 was a step-change in FY2026 guidance to at least $375 million. To put that value into perspective, that figure is more than double the company’s prior $170 million to $180 million outlook issued only days earlier and implies roughly 7x growth versus FY2025 revenue of $50.7 million.

While the bears are arguing about the quality of that growth, I am not overly concerned with the recent acquisition spree. Management disclosed that $230 million, or 61% of the FY26 guidance, comes from the recent 5 acquisitions in Q1 2026, leaving roughly $145 million from the pre-Q1 2026 acquisition base. Even though that figure is not pure organic revenue, as it still includes contributions from last year's acquisitions, the organic revenue growth in Q4 was 63% YOY, a decent figure in my view.

That's just the tip of the iceberg of my bull case on Ondas and the reason why I upgraded to a Buy. Below, I explain more.

A Revenue Inflection Point

By far, the main highlight in Q4 earnings was the FY 2026 revenue guidance of at least $375 million.

For reference, just a few days prior to the Q4 release, the company guided $170 million-$180 million in FY 2026 revenue, excluding the impact of any new acquisitions announced in 2026.

In other words, in just a few days, the company more than doubled its FY26 outlook, although bear in mind that the latest guidance includes the list from acquisitions.

When comparing the $375M projected for FY26 with the revenue in 2025 of $50.7 million, the increase is 7x.

Interestingly, the company provided a very detailed breakdown of the contribution to FY26 revenue of each of its 5 recent acquisitions. Specifically, $230 million of the >$375 million guide comes from the Q1 2026 acquisitions.

Notably, the company exited Q4 with only $68.3 million in backlog. In other words, the backlog covers only about 18% of the FY26 guide floor. This means that the company is betting on a massive increase in orders this year.

As you may know, the company has a product offering beyond just drones, so the backlog is exposed to multiple product lines. For reference, the company offers a scaled autonomous defense platform, including counter-UAS, intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR), loitering munition systems, and unmanned ground vehicles (UGVs).

One of the areas that has received a lot of attention recently is the counter-UAS segment. Earlier this month, the company announced approximately $6 million of new orders for dozens of counter-drone systems from current defense and homeland security customers in the Middle East and other regions. As noted during the earnings call, CEO Ryan Hartman noted some preparation work for operations like Epic Fury:

We are also actively preparing for the inclusion of our technology in support of active U.S. Department of War operations like Epic Fury.

Still within the counter-UAS segment, the company announced in February a new multi-million-dollar European order for Iron Drone Raider. Unfortunately, Ondas didn't disclose the exact figure, although the PR noted that it was part of a prior $16.8 million in orders to protect two major European international airports.

Still within Europe, Sentrycs (an Israeli counter-UAS company acquired at the end of last year) disclosed in February a deployment with a German State Police office, with more than 1,000 suspicious drone flights in Germany during 2025. Interestingly, the Sentrycs technology was used within Swisscom Broadcast’s DroneDefence deployment at the World Economic Forum in Davos.

The other product line that I like is intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR).

Earlier this month, the company announced a $10 million investment in World View (a high-altitude balloon ISR and stratospheric remote sensing company). Just a few days later, Palantir, Ondas, and World View announced a strategic partnership to build an AI-enabled multi-domain ISR program (well, it's actually three programs: Warp Speed, AI Flight Director, and SkyWeaver).

From a timing perspective, the PR said that the integration across Ondas’ broader portfolio could begin as early as Q4 2026.

Based on the significant jump in the Street's revenue models below, I can conclude that Q4 earnings were an inflection point in how the market perceives this company.

Risks

Top-line growth is driven by acquisitions, which made me wonder how much organic growth is behind the revenue figures.

The income statement within the latest investor deck shows the exact figure: 63% YOY organic growth in Q4 (vs. 629% YOY including acquisitions).

Undoubtedly, there is a massive gap between organic and inorganic growth. Therefore, I would closely monitor this metric in the next earnings reports.

I provide below my estimate for organic growth this year.

Management guided >$375M in FY26 revenue, including the new acquisitions this year. As I've shown in the last section, $230M of the $375M comes from these recent acquisitions alone. So, the implied 2026 revenue excluding the five 2026 acquisitions is about $145 million, equal to roughly 186% growth versus the total revenue in 2025.

Not bad, although that value downplays the 7x YOY growth after considering the contributions from the 2026 acquisitions.

Looking Ahead

I remain bullish on the company, and I upgrade my rating to a Strong Buy.

Valuation has nothing to do with my upgrade. In fact, if one looks at the 12.6x forward P/S multiple, one may easily conclude this stock is grotesquely overvalued and move on.

My argument is that the guidance reset in Q4 was a true inflection point for the company. In my view, Ondas is now re-rating from a small drone story into what I see as a scaled autonomous defense platform, with offerings like counter-UAS, ISR, and unmanned ground vehicles. That opens the potential for multiple contracts on both sides of the Atlantic.

In the U.S. specifically, the main tailwind that I see is the $1.5T U.S. defense budget for 2027 proposed by Trump earlier this year. For reference, the 2026 budget was $901B, so we're talking about a $500B potential increase in just a year. I strongly believe part of that excess budget will spill over to drone programs, and I see Ondas as a strong beneficiary, particularly in its counter-UAS segment.

In the meantime, profitability is still far away. In the last earnings report, Ondas said Q1 2026 adjusted EBITDA losses are expected to increase, while company-wide profitability is still targeted for Q1 2028. In the meantime, I think the cash and cash equivalents position of $1.55 billion is more than enough considering the FY 2025 cash outflow of just $38.75M.

Overall, I see Ondas as a Strong Buy, although I expect the recent stock's volatility to persist until the conflict in Iran comes to an end.

data.title: GUT Is Good, But XLU Is Better
data.description:
Introduction

At the end of last year, I predicted that 2026 would be an anemic year. So far, my predictions have been too true. I am also anticipating rocky years for 2027-2029. With this in mind, I wanted to identify investments that could help us weather the coming storm. My initial search led me to the Gabelli Utility Trust (GUT), but I also wanted to look at the broader index. After I did that, I believed the State Street Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU) was a better choice.

About The Gabelli Utility Trust

Here are some factoids about GUT to help one understand how it operates.

Universe

Formed in July 1999, The Gabelli Utility invests in companies that generate or distribute electricity, gas, water, and telecommunications services, as well as in infrastructure. Its benchmarks are the S&P 500 Utilities Index and the Lipper Utility Fund Average.

Investment Process

GUT is not an index fund. GUT focuses on companies with regulated cash flows and strong balance sheets. The cash flow must be durable. Earnings must be predictable with rate-based growth, and the companies must have durable asset bases and assured return on equity. Relative to their peers, the companies need to have good value. To generate income for investors, GUT prefers companies with secure dividends.

Portfolio Construction

80% of GUT’s assets are invested in utilities, both domestic and foreign. I like the low turnover (3%) and that it is not overly concentrated in the top ten holdings. These are the top holdings:

Risk Concerns

Distributions

GUT pays a monthly distribution, not a dividend. The current distribution rate is 10.34% annually. It is not a dividend because it can include income, capital gains, and other sources.

data.title: Gilead: Why The 'Immune System Company' Pivot Is No Longer A Bargain (Downgrade)
data.description:
Gilead Overview

Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD) has been a beneficiary of a big pharma rally these past few months.

Although I cannot so much speak for the others, I feel confident that Gilead’s stock appreciation has been driven by fundamentals.

When I last visited Gilead in Sept. 2025 (rating upgrade to Buy), I contrasted a seemingly pessimistic valuation (with implied assumptions of -1% CAGR and 32% FCF margins) with its impressively steady multi-billion-dollar HIV franchise (e.g., Yeztugo) and growing presence in oncology (e.g., Trodelvy). I noted that should GILD’s growth assumption bump to merely 4%, its per share value could increase to $162.28.

Its stock came awfully close ($157.29) to $162.28 earlier this year before settling under $140/share.

I see that Gilead has since reported FY25 earnings and has been active in M&A (Ouro Medicines). So, I wanted to take another look to see if it’s still worth buying here.

FY25 Performance

Gilead’s main play continues to be HIV. Due to pricing pressures, the company is tasked with transitioning its portfolio from daily pills (i.e., Descovy) to its long-acting injectables (i.e., Yeztugo).

This isn’t just about money, though. Long-acting injectables are also better for patients. Treatment compliance is paramount in HIV.

So, Gilead’s eggs are still very much in the “HIV basket,” but, as I pointed out last year, Trodelvy continues to shine.

In January, Trodelvy became a preferred (NCCN Category 1) first-line option for mTNBC patients. This was big because, until just recently, Trodelvy was reserved for second-line or later.

Analysts expect Trodelvy to reach $3B in peak annual revenue, so it could support growth for quite a while.

Gilead’s “sore thumb” continues to be its Cell Therapy segment. Its Kite-enabled first-mover advantage is being eroded by newer technologies. Specifically, off-the-shelf bispecifics like AbbVie’s (ABBV) Epkinly and Roche’s (RHHBY) Columvi offer quick turnarounds. Whereas Gilead’s Yescarta requires a patient’s cells to be shipped to a lab, modified, and flown back. This can take up to 17 days, which is a long time when you have an aggressive cancer.

Gilead’s CAR-Ts are also facing competition from other CAR-Ts, like Bristol-Myers Squibb’s Breyanzi. Breyanzi is increasingly favored due its relative safety benefits.

Its solution is to replace its legacy CAR-T products with anito-cel, a next-gen therapy targeting BMCA for multiple myeloma that was acquired for $7.8B from Arcellx. Gilead is also investing in in vivo CAR-T via its earlier $350M acquisition of Interius BioTherapeutics.

Gilead’s most recent acquisition of Ouro Medicines adds OM336, a bispecific antibody (TCE). This one is going after a completely different market than oncology and HIV: autoimmune diseases like Lupus and ITP. By targeting BCMA to deplete B-cells, the hope is to cause a permanent “immune reset” rather than daily immunosuppression.

So Gilead is really going after “big ideas” in the “B-cell depletion” market, which could have multiple applications.

While I like these moves from Gilead (they are effectively trying to become an “Immune System Company”), I don’t think they are coming from a position of strength. Due to limited diversity and ongoing headwinds in Cell Therapy, Gilead has no choice but to make changes.

That said, a giant and stable HIV franchise, combined with a grower in Trodelvy, gives Gilead a solid ground to move on. So that these acquisitions do not appear entirely out of desperation.

Valuation Assumptions

Because GILD is 20% more expensive since my Sept. 2025 analysis, I wanted to revisit its valuation assumptions.

First, I want to get an idea of what Gilead has been doing these past eight quarters.

When it comes to trends, nothing in particular sticks out to me. Gilead’s average FCF margin is 33.3%. Its average revenue growth rate is 2.2%, but has improved in recent periods.

This informs my DCF model assumptions.

Year 1 Revenue of $30B is Gilead’s official guidance for FY26.

I modeled for 3.3% (on average) Revenue Growth with a Moderate Decline Growth Profile (Yrs 1–3: 4.0% → Yrs 4–6: 3.3% → Yrs 7–9: 2.6%). This comes out to a realized CAGR of 3.2%.

FCF Margin of 32% appears a bit conservative compared to Gilead’s last eight quarters, but I suspect that its M&A spree will result in higher R&D investments. So, I wanted to model for some buffer there.

A Beta of 0.8 ("normalized forward") is pretty typical for a large pharma stock, and accounts for pipeline risk and capital intensity.

Most everything else is either standard DCF methodology or was derived from Gilead’s most recent financial statements.

Given that my DCF Value per Share of $138.39 falls within a dime and three pennies within the Current Stock Price, this is my “Base” case for GILD.

Scenario Analysis

But what would a “Bear” or “Bull” case look like?

In the “Bear” case, the realized CAGR drops to 2.4%, which is nearly identical to GILD’s 2% Terminal Growth assumption. Its FCF Margin drops considerably to 28% to reflect increasing pricing pressure and R&D expenditures. GILD’s per share value drops to $107.54.

In the “Bull” case, the realized CAGR jumps to 4.5% with a Peak Mid-Cycle Growth Profile (Yrs 1–3: 3.2% → Yrs 4–6: 6.9% → Yrs 7–9: 3.7%). This will need some clinical trial wins/major drug launches. FCF Margin also inches forward to 34%. And, given key pipeline advancements, GILD’s Terminal Growth assumption jumps to 2.5%.

Reverse DCF Analysis

Going back to my “Base” case, which aligns very closely to implied market assumptions, there is really a combination of variables that can result in the current market price of $138.26.

For example, let’s say GILD’s average Revenue Growth falls to 2.3%. Then it would need 34% FCF Margins to support its current valuation.

It's not detailed in the image, but growth moves GILD's value nearly 2x that of margin improvements. That is, for every 1pp improvement in growth, GILD's valuation jumps ~7%. Whereas it jumps ~3.5% for every 1pp improvement in margin.

Monte Carlo Simulation

Finally, my Monte Carlo simulation displays 10,000 simulations of my “Base” case…to give us an idea of what GILD can trade between before it becomes unreasonably over- or undervalued (assuming no material changes to base assumptions).

I like to compare these results ($98.03 to $209.40/share) to GILD’s 52-week range ($93.37 to $157.29/share).

The model’s 5th and 95th percentile values ($98.03 and $209.40/share) represent a realistic trading range for GILD. GILD could reasonably trade near $98/share without becoming majorly undervalued. It could be near $200/share without being overvalued. Again, this is barring major changes to key assumptions (i.e., realized CAGR of 3.2% → 2% Terminal). In one example, if anito-cel becomes the de facto SoC in MM, GILD’s growth assumptions throughout the projection period would be materially higher, lifting this “range.”

Conclusion

In its current state, GILD appears appropriately valued. The market has picked up on Gilead’s strengthening HIV franchise fortress and Trodelvy’s outperformance since my last look. But there’s a lot of reason for caution, too. Gilead is leaning heavy on M&A expenditure to “stop the bleeding” from their Cell Therapy segment (which was also acquired). Its “B-cell depletion for all” strategy is uncertain and may not ever pay off. And, after all, despite the appeal of its generous 2.4% dividend yield, even with the stock’s recent rally, GILD is up merely 44% in the past ten years!

So, I’m happy to lock in these gains and downgrade GILD to Hold pending major clinical developments.
